title: Topic 139: The Psychology of Crowdfunding Games: Hope, Hype, and Disappointment
content:
### ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the psychological rollercoaster that is crowdfunding for video games, a realm where dreams are bought, sold, and occasionally, delivered. We explore the potent cocktail of hope, hype, and herd behavior that compels rational adults to invest in digital promises. Through a rigorous, albeit amusing, analysis of consumer psychology, we dissect the mechanisms that turn a well-pitched Kickstarter campaign into a multi-million dollar phenomenon, and how, with equal predictability, it can devolve into a vortex of disappointment and internet rage. This study serves as a cautionary tale for the aspiring backer, a field guide for the curious academic, and a source of schadenfreude for everyone else. We will examine the halo effect, social proof, and the particular strain of amnesia that seems to afflict the gaming community, causing them to repeatedly fall for the same tantalizingly unobtainable digital carrots. In short, we’re here to ask: why do we keep giving money to people who promise us the moon on a stick, and why are we so surprised when all we get is the stick?

### INTRODUCTION

In the grand casino of the digital age, few games of chance are as thrilling or as potentially soul-crushing as the video game Kickstarter. It is a utopian bazaar where starry-eyed developers and enthusiastic gamers converge, a place where the currency is not just money, but hope. Hope for a game that will finally deliver on its promises, hope for a return to the golden age of gaming, and hope that this time, just this once, the finished product will look even remotely like the concept art. This paper will embark on a perilous journey into the heart of this phenomenon, exploring the psychological underpinnings of why we, as a species, are so willing to gamble on the future of fun.

Crowdfunding has democratized game development, allowing small, independent studios to compete with industry giants. It has also, however, created a new and fascinating ecosystem of consumer behavior, one that is ripe for academic, and comedic, scrutiny. We will investigate the irresistible allure of the “stretch goal,” the hypnotic power of a charismatic developer, and the collective amnesia that allows us to forget the smoldering craters of past Kickstarter disasters. From the dizzying highs of a successfully funded project to the crushing lows of a delayed, downgraded, or altogether abandoned game, we will chart the emotional trajectory of the modern backer.

This is not just a story about video games. It is a story about human nature, about our eternal optimism, our susceptibility to persuasion, and our desperate, often irrational, desire to be part of something new and exciting. So, grab your wallets and your skepticism, and join us as we explore the psychology of crowdfunding games. It’s going to be a bumpy, but hopefully entertaining, ride.

### LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the peculiar habits of the digital consumer. While the specific sub-genre of "the psychology of throwing money at video game promises" is still emerging, a wealth of existing research provides a sturdy, if slightly dusty, framework for our investigation. This section will bravely venture into the hallowed halls of academia, armed with a flask of coffee and a healthy dose of irreverence, to see what the great minds of psychology have to say about our questionable crowdfunding habits.

One of the most potent weapons in the Kickstarter creator's arsenal is the **halo effect**, a cognitive bias first identified by Thorndike in 1920, and later explored in the context of personality by Asch. The halo effect, in essence, is the tendency for an initial positive impression of a person, brand, or, in our case, a Kickstarter pitch, to positively influence one's perception of their other traits. A slickly produced video, featuring a developer with a charming accent and a tastefully curated collection of vintage synthesizers, can create a powerful halo effect. Suddenly, the project seems more likely to succeed, the gameplay mechanics more innovative, and the estimated delivery date less like a work of pure fiction. As the saying goes, "what is beautiful is good," and in the world of Kickstarter, a beautiful pitch is a successful one.

Then there is the small matter of **social proof**, a psychological phenomenon that can be summarized as "everyone else is doing it, so it must be a good idea." This is the principle that drives lemmings off cliffs and, with slightly less fatal consequences, backers to Kickstarter campaigns. A 2006 study by Salganik, Dodds, and Watts on an artificial music market demonstrated the power of social influence. They found that the popularity of a song was not just a matter of its intrinsic quality, but was heavily influenced by how many other people had downloaded it. The same principle applies to Kickstarter. A project with a high number of backers and a rapidly growing funding total is like a siren's call to the undecided consumer. "12,435 backers can't be wrong, can they?" we ask ourselves, conveniently ignoring the fact that, historically, large groups of people have been wrong about a great many things.

Finally, we must consider the role of **psychological reactance**. This is the delightful human tendency to want something more when we are told we can't have it. It's the reason why "limited edition" anything is so effective, and why the bouncer at an exclusive nightclub is your new best friend. Kickstarter campaigns are masters of psychological reactance. Stretch goals, early bird specials, and exclusive backer rewards all create a sense of urgency and scarcity. "Back now, or forever hold your peace (and your money)," they whisper seductively. This fear of missing out (FOMO) is a powerful motivator, and it can be the difference between a project that languishes in obscurity and one that becomes a runaway success.

### ANALYSIS

Now that we have armed ourselves with the theoretical underpinnings of our investigation, it is time to descend into the digital trenches and examine the real-world application of these psychological principles. In this section, we will dissect the anatomy of a successful, and not-so-successful, Kickstarter campaign, and explore the intricate dance of hope, hype, and human fallibility that defines the crowdfunding experience.

The initial pitch is where the magic, and the manipulation, begins. A well-crafted Kickstarter campaign is a masterclass in persuasive storytelling. It is not just about selling a product; it is about selling a dream. The developer is not just a programmer; they are a visionary, a passionate artist who has been toiling away in their digital garret, fueled by nothing but ramen noodles and a burning desire to create the game of your dreams. The pitch video is the centerpiece of this narrative, a carefully constructed piece of propaganda designed to elicit an emotional response. It will feature sweeping orchestral music, tantalizing glimpses of concept art, and a developer who speaks with the earnest conviction of a prophet.

The rewards system is another key component of the Kickstarter ecosystem. It is a carefully calibrated system of incentives designed to encourage backers to part with their hard-earned cash. The rewards are often tiered, with each successive tier offering a more enticing array of digital and physical goodies. A small pledge might get you a digital copy of the game, while a larger pledge could earn you a signed poster, a t-shirt, or even the chance to have your name immortalized in the game's credits. This system taps into our innate desire for status and recognition. It allows us to feel like we are not just consumers, but patrons of the arts, and that our contribution, no matter how small, is valued.

But what happens when the hype train derails? What happens when the promises of the pitch video collide with the harsh reality of game development? This is where the psychology of disappointment comes into play. The same psychological principles that make Kickstarter so successful can also make it a breeding ground for bitterness and resentment. The halo effect can quickly tarnish when the developer, once seen as a visionary, is now perceived as a charlatan. Social proof can turn into a collective sense of betrayal, as backers realize that they were not part of a wise investment, but a foolish herd. And psychological reactance can morph into a burning desire for refunds and retribution.

The cautionary tale of *Mighty No. 9* serves as a perfect example of this phenomenon. Billed as the spiritual successor to the beloved *Mega Man* series, and helmed by the legendary Keiji Inafune, the game's Kickstarter campaign was a resounding success, raising over $3.8 million. However, the final product was a critical and commercial disappointment, a pale imitation of the game that was promised. The backlash from backers was swift and brutal, a testament to the power of shattered expectations. The story of *Mighty No. 9* is a stark reminder that in the world of crowdfunding, the line between hope and hype is a thin one, and the fall from grace can be a long and painful one.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

To truly understand the dizzying highs and crushing lows of the crowdfunding rollercoaster, we must examine the real-world case studies that have become the stuff of internet legend. These are the stories of dreams realized and hopes dashed, of promises kept and expectations shattered. These are the tales from the Kickstarter crypt.

**The Success Stories: When Hope Springs Eternal (and Delivers)**

For every crowdfunding cautionary tale, there is a story of a project that not only met but exceeded its backers' expectations. These are the games that serve as a beacon of hope for the entire crowdfunding ecosystem, proof that sometimes, just sometimes, dreams do come true.

*   ***Shovel Knight:*** A love letter to the 8-bit era, *Shovel Knight* is the poster child for a successful Kickstarter campaign. The game's developer, Yacht Club Games, was transparent and communicative throughout the development process, and the final product was a critical and commercial success. It is a shining example of how to do crowdfunding right.

*   ***Pillars of Eternity:*** Developed by the RPG masters at Obsidian Entertainment, *Pillars of Eternity* was a nostalgia-fueled trip back to the golden age of computer RPGs. The game's Kickstarter campaign was a massive success, and the final product was a deep, engaging, and critically acclaimed RPG that delivered on its promises.

*   ***Undertale:*** A quirky and innovative RPG that defied genre conventions, *Undertale* was a surprise hit that was funded by a modest Kickstarter campaign. The game's creator, Toby Fox, was a one-man development team, and his passion and creativity shone through in the final product. *Undertale* is a testament to the power of a unique vision and a dedicated community.

**The Cautionary Tales: When Hype Meets Reality (and Reality Wins)**

And then there are the other stories. The stories of projects that promised the world and delivered a buggy, unfinished mess, or in some cases, nothing at all. These are the games that have become synonymous with Kickstarter failure, the digital ghosts that haunt the crowdfunding landscape.

*   ***Mighty No. 9:*** As mentioned previously, *Mighty No. 9* is the quintessential example of a Kickstarter campaign that failed to live up to the hype. The game's development was plagued by delays and mismanagement, and the final product was a soulless imitation of the classic *Mega Man* games. The backlash from backers was a harsh lesson in the dangers of over-promising and under-delivering.

*   ***Godus:*** From the legendary game designer Peter Molyneux, *Godus* was supposed to be a revolutionary "god game" that would allow players to shape a world and its inhabitants. The Kickstarter campaign was a success, but the game was released in a broken and unfinished state, and Molyneux eventually abandoned the project, leaving backers with a digital paperweight.

*   ***The Stomping Land:*** A multiplayer survival game featuring dinosaurs, *The Stomping Land* had a hugely successful Kickstarter campaign, raising over $114,000. However, the game's development was a disaster, and the developer eventually disappeared, taking the backers' money with him. The game was eventually removed from Steam, and it remains one of the most infamous scams in Kickstarter history.

### DISCUSSION

The tumultuous world of video game crowdfunding offers a fascinating window into the complexities of modern consumer behavior. The stories of success and failure, of hope and disappointment, are not just entertaining anecdotes; they are rich data points that can help us understand the psychological forces that shape our purchasing decisions. In this section, we will discuss the broader implications of our findings, and explore what the psychology of crowdfunding can teach us about ourselves.

One of the most striking takeaways from our analysis is the power of narrative. A successful Kickstarter campaign is not just about selling a product; it is about selling a story. The most successful campaigns are those that can create a compelling narrative, one that resonates with backers on an emotional level. This is a lesson that extends far beyond the world of video games. In an increasingly crowded and competitive marketplace, the ability to tell a good story is a crucial skill for any brand or business.

The Kickstarter phenomenon also highlights the growing importance of community in the digital age. Backers are not just passive consumers; they are active participants in the creative process. They are investors, evangelists, and, in some cases, co-creators. This sense of community is a powerful motivator, and it can be a key factor in the success of a crowdfunding campaign. The lesson for businesses is clear: in the 21st century, it is not enough to have customers; you need to have a community.

Finally, the cautionary tales of Kickstarter failure serve as a stark reminder of the importance of trust and transparency. In the world of crowdfunding, a developer's reputation is their most valuable asset. A single broken promise can be enough to shatter the trust of an entire community, and once that trust is gone, it is almost impossible to win back. The message for creators is simple: be honest, be transparent, and above all, be worthy of your backers' trust.

### CONCLUSION

In the end, what can we conclude from our whirlwind tour of the psychology of crowdfunding games? We have seen that the decision to back a Kickstarter project is a complex and often irrational one, driven by a potent cocktail of hope, hype, and herd behavior. We have explored the psychological principles that make us so susceptible to the siren's call of a well-pitched campaign, and we have witnessed the devastating consequences of shattered expectations.

But perhaps the most important lesson is this: for all its flaws and foibles, the world of crowdfunding is a fundamentally optimistic one. It is a world where people are willing to take a chance on a dream, to invest in the future of fun. And while there will always be disappointments and disasters, there will also be triumphs and successes, moments of collective joy that remind us why we were so willing to take a chance in the first place. So, the next time you find yourself hovering over the "back this project" button, take a moment to consider the psychological forces at play. And then, if you're feeling lucky, go ahead and click. After all, you never know. This time, you might just get the moon on a stick.
references:
[1] Asch, S. E. (1946). Forming impressions of personality. *The Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology, 41*(3), 258–290.

[2] Salganik, M. J., Dodds, P. S., & Watts, D. J. (2006). Experimental study of inequality and unpredictability in an artificial cultural market. *Science, 311*(5762), 854-856.

[3] Thorndike, E. L. (1920). A constant error in psychological ratings. *Journal of Applied Psychology, 4*(1), 25–29.

[4] The Psychology of Games. (2015, March 24). *Psychology and Video Game Kickstarters*. Retrieved from https://www.psychologyofgames.com/2015/03/psychology-and-kickstarters/

[5] The Gamer. (2023, July 4). *The 10 Most Successful Video Game Kickstarters*. Retrieved from https://www.thegamer.com/most-successful-video-game-kickstarters/
